questions.question: The chromatic number of a graph is the minimum number of colours used in a proper colouring of the graph. The chromatic number of the following graph is __________. [The figure shows a graph on 8 vertices: a top row of 4 vertices and a bottom row of 4 vertices, with horizontal edges and several crossing diagonal edges between the two rows, drawn so that every edge joins a top vertex to a bottom vertex.]
questions.to_find: Minimum number of colours for a proper colouring (chromatic number)

understand.plain: Chromatic number chi(G) is the fewest colours needed so that no two adjacent vertices share a colour. If a graph has no odd cycle, it is bipartite and just 2 colours suffice (as long as it has at least one edge). Look closely: every edge in this figure runs between the top group and the bottom group, never within a group, so the graph is bipartite.

keywords.explain: An assignment of colours to vertices such that the two endpoints of every edge get different colours.
keywords.example: Colouring all top vertices red and all bottom vertices blue is proper if no edge joins two top (or two bottom) vertices.

keywords.explain: A graph whose vertices split into two sets so that every edge goes between the sets; equivalently, a graph with no odd-length cycle.
keywords.example: Here the top 4 vertices and bottom 4 vertices form the two sides, so the graph is bipartite.

keywords.explain: The smallest number of colours in any proper colouring of G.
keywords.example: A single edge needs 2 colours; an empty graph needs 1; a triangle needs 3.

cx='60' cy='190' r='9' class='ok'/><circle cx='180' cy='190' r='9' class='ok'/><circle cx='300' cy='190' r='9' class='ok'/><circle cx='420' cy='190' r='9' class='ok'/><text class='tag ink' x='30' y='40'>Colour A (top)</text><text class='tag ink' x='30' y='235'>Colour B (bottom)</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Eight vertices in a top row and a bottom row; every edge joins a top vertex to a bottom vertex, so two colours (one per row) suffice.

given.aim: Find the smallest number of colours that properly colours this 8-vertex graph.

terms.meaning: The 8 dots of the graph, naturally split into a top set of 4 and a bottom set of 4.
terms.connects: These two sets form the bipartition that proves chi = 2.

terms.meaning: The lines drawn; each line joins a top vertex to a bottom vertex.
terms.example: Horizontal links plus crossing diagonals, none of them within a single row.
terms.connects: No edge inside a part means no odd cycle, hence bipartite.

given.plan: Show the graph is bipartite (two sides with no internal edges, equivalently no odd cycle), then use chi = 2 for any bipartite graph that has at least one edge.

steps.title: Identify the two parts
steps.formula_raw: V = T union B, every edge goes T <-> B
steps.apply: Label the 4 top vertices as set T and the 4 bottom vertices as set B. Tracing every drawn edge (horizontals and crossing diagonals), each one connects a vertex of T to a vertex of B; no edge joins two T vertices or two B vertices.
steps.note: A 2-part split with all edges across the parts is exactly the definition of a bipartite graph.

steps.title: Lower bound: at least 2 colours
steps.apply: The graph clearly contains edges, so one colour cannot work (an edge's two endpoints must differ). Hence chi(G) >= 2.
steps.note: Only an edgeless graph can be 1-coloured.

steps.title: Upper bound: 2 colours suffice
steps.apply: Colour every top vertex A and every bottom vertex B. Since each edge runs between T and B, its endpoints get different colours, so this is a proper colouring with 2 colours. Therefore chi(G) <= 2.

questions.formula_note: The key fact is that a graph with at least one edge is 2-colourable exactly when it is bipartite (no odd cycle); this figure's edges all cross between two vertex sets, so chi = 2.

questions.question: Consider the undirected graph G defined as follows. The vertices of G are bit strings of length n. We have an edge between vertex u and vertex v if and only if u and v differ in exactly one bit position (in other words, v can be obtained from u by flipping a single bit). The ratio of the chromatic number of G to the diameter of G is, A. 1/(2^(n-1)) B. (1/n) C. (2/n) D. (3/n)
questions.to_find: Ratio chi(G) / diameter(G) for the n-bit hypercube graph

understand.plain: The described graph is the n-dimensional hypercube Q_n: vertices are length-n bit strings and two are adjacent when they differ in exactly one bit. Q_n is bipartite (split by parity of the number of 1s), so its chromatic number is 2. Its diameter equals n because the farthest pair (a string and its complement) differs in all n bits and you must flip them one at a time. So the ratio is 2/n.

keywords.explain: Graph on all 2^n bit strings of length n; edges join strings that differ in one bit.
keywords.example: Q_2 is a 4-cycle 00-01-11-10-00; Q_3 is a cube.

keywords.explain: The number of bit positions in which two strings differ; it equals the shortest-path distance in Q_n.
keywords.example: 000 and 011 differ in 2 bits, so their distance is 2.

keywords.explain: The largest shortest-path distance between any two vertices.
keywords.example: In Q_n the string 00...0 and 11...1 are n apart, the maximum, so diameter = n.

cx='130' cy='50' r='8' class='hi'/><circle cx='270' cy='50' r='8' class='ok'/><circle cx='130' cy='190' r='8' class='ok'/><circle cx='270' cy='190' r='8' class='hi'/><text class='tag ink' x='40' y='85'>000</text><text class='tag ink' x='278' y='55'>111</text><text class='tag mut' x='30' y='285'>Q_3: 2-colourable, diameter 3</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: The 3-bit hypercube Q_3 drawn as a cube, two-coloured by parity of bit-sum, with 000 and 111 a distance of 3 apart.

given.aim: Compute chi(G)/diameter(G) for the n-dimensional hypercube G = Q_n.

terms.meaning: Vertices are all 2^n bit strings of length n; edges join strings differing in one bit.
terms.example: For n = 3 this is the ordinary cube graph.
terms.connects: All the structure (bipartite, distance) follows from this definition.

terms.meaning: Diameter of the hypercube, the maximum Hamming distance.

given.plan: Use parity of the bit-sum to 2-colour Q_n (so chi = 2), use that shortest-path distance equals Hamming distance to get diameter = n, then divide.

steps.title: Chromatic number of Q_n
steps.apply: Colour each bit string by the parity of its number of 1s (even = colour 0, odd = colour 1). Flipping one bit changes the parity, so every edge joins an even-parity vertex to an odd-parity vertex. This is a proper 2-colouring, and since edges exist, chi(Q_n) = 2.
steps.note: Equivalently, Q_n has no odd cycle, the hallmark of a bipartite graph.

steps.title: Diameter of Q_n
steps.apply: The shortest path from u to v flips, one at a time, exactly the bits where they differ, so distance(u,v) equals the Hamming distance. The maximum Hamming distance is n, achieved by a string and its complement (e.g. 00...0 and 11...1). Hence diam(Q_n) = n.
steps.note: You cannot fix more than one differing bit per edge, so n flips are unavoidable.

steps.title: Form the ratio
steps.apply: Divide: chi(G)/diam(G) = 2/n, which is option C.

questions.formula_note: The decisive idea is that the hypercube Q_n is bipartite (chi = 2) while its diameter equals n (the maximum Hamming distance), giving the ratio 2/n.

questions.question: What is the chromatic number of the following graph? [The figure is a 3 x 3 grid of 9 vertices with the usual horizontal and vertical grid edges, plus one extra curved edge that arcs over the top and down the right side joining the top-left corner vertex to the bottom-right corner vertex.] A. 2 B. 3 C. 4 D. 5
questions.to_find: Chromatic number of the 3 x 3 grid with one extra corner-to-corner edge

understand.plain: A plain 3 x 3 grid is bipartite (colour it like a checkerboard), so on its own it needs only 2 colours. With a checkerboard colouring, both the top-left corner and the bottom-right corner get the SAME colour. The extra arc joins exactly those two corners, so they can no longer share a colour, forcing a third colour. Three colours then suffice, so the chromatic number is 3.

keywords.explain: Vertices placed on a lattice with edges between horizontal and vertical neighbours; it is bipartite.
keywords.example: Colour vertex (r,c) by the parity of r + c to 2-colour any grid.

keywords.explain: A cycle with an odd number of edges; a graph needs 3 or more colours exactly when it contains one.
keywords.example: The arc plus a grid path between the two corners forms an odd-length cycle here.

keywords.explain: Colour (r,c) by (r+c) mod 2; corners (0,0) and (2,2) both give 0, the same colour.
keywords.example: Top-left and bottom-right are both 'black' squares.

xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe class='hi'/><circle cx='240' cy='90' r='8' class='ok'/><circle cx='80' cy='170' r='8' class='hi'/><circle cx='160' cy='170' r='8' class='ok'/><circle cx='240' cy='170' r='8' class='hi'/><circle cx='80' cy='250' r='8' class='ok'/><circle cx='160' cy='250' r='8' class='hi'/><circle cx='240' cy='250' r='8' class='hi'/><text class='tag mut' x='30' y='300'>arc joins two same-parity corners -> need 3 colours</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: A 3 by 3 grid with an extra arc joining the top-left and bottom-right corners; those corners share a checkerboard colour, so a third colour is required.

given.aim: Find the chromatic number of the 3 x 3 grid graph plus the extra corner-to-corner arc.

terms.meaning: Nine vertices in rows r = 0,1,2 and columns c = 0,1,2.
terms.connects: Their parity r+c drives the 2-colouring of the grid.

terms.meaning: Edges between horizontally and vertically adjacent vertices.
terms.connects: These alone make a bipartite (2-colourable) graph.

terms.meaning: The curved edge joining corner (0,0) to corner (2,2).
terms.example: Drawn outside the grid, over the top and down the right.
terms.connects: Joins two same-colour corners, creating an odd cycle and forcing chi = 3.

given.plan: 2-colour the grid by parity, observe the arc joins two same-colour corners (creating an odd cycle), so chi >= 3; then exhibit a proper 3-colouring to show chi = 3.

steps.title: Two colours fail because of the arc
steps.formula_raw: G has an odd cycle  =>  chi(G) >= 3
steps.apply: Colour the grid by parity: vertex (r,c) gets colour (r+c) mod 2. Then (0,0) and (2,2) both get colour 0. The extra arc joins (0,0) to (2,2); together with any grid path between them (length 4, even) it forms a cycle of odd total length 5, so the graph is not bipartite and chi >= 3.
steps.note: Any 2-colouring of the grid forces these two corners equal, which the arc forbids.

steps.title: Three colours are enough
steps.apply: Keep the parity colouring (colours 1 and 2) for the eight vertices other than (2,2), and give the bottom-right corner (2,2) a new colour 3. Now every grid edge still has differently coloured endpoints, and the arc (0,0)-(2,2) joins colour 1 to colour 3. All edges are properly coloured with 3 colours, so chi <= 3.
steps.note: Only the single conflicting corner needed recolouring.

steps.title: Combine the bounds
steps.apply: The two bounds pin the chromatic number to exactly 3, which is option B.

questions.formula_note: The key idea is that the grid is bipartite but the extra corner-to-corner arc creates an odd cycle (the two corners share a colour in any 2-colouring), so the chromatic number rises from 2 to exactly 3.

questions.question: Specify an adjacency-lists representation of the undirected graph given above. [The graph has 5 vertices labelled 1,2,3,4,5. Vertex 2 is at the top; 1 (left) and 3 (right) are in the middle; 5 (bottom-left) and 4 (bottom-right) are at the base. The edges shown are: 1-2, 2-3, 1-3, 1-5, 3-4, 4-5, and the diagonal 3-5.]
questions.to_find: Adjacency-list representation of the 5-vertex undirected graph

understand.plain: An adjacency list stores, for each vertex, the set of vertices directly joined to it by an edge. For an undirected graph each edge {u,v} appears twice: v is listed under u and u is listed under v. We simply read off the seven edges from the figure and write the neighbour set of each of the five vertices.

keywords.explain: A representation that, for every vertex, keeps a list of its adjacent (neighbouring) vertices.
keywords.example: If vertex 2 is joined to 1 and 3, its list is 2 -> {1, 3}.

keywords.explain: An unordered pair {u,v}; it contributes u to v's list and v to u's list.
keywords.example: Edge {1,5} puts 5 in list of 1 and 1 in list of 5.

keywords.explain: The number of neighbours of a vertex, i.e. the length of its adjacency list.

understand.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 300 280' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><line class='edge' x1='150' y1='40' x2='70' y2='110' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2.5'/><line class='edge' x1='150' y1='40' x2='230' y2='110' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2.5'/><line class='edge' x1='70' y1='110' x2='230' y2='110' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2.5'/><line class='edge' x1='70' y1='110' x2='70' y2='220' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2.5'/><line class='edge' x1='230' y1='110' x2='230' y2='220' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2.5'/><line class='edge' x1='70' y1='220' x2='230' y2='220' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2.5'/><line class='edge' x1='70' y1='220' x2='230' y2='110' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2.5'/><circle cx='150' cy='40' r='11' class='hi'/><circle cx='70' cy='110' r='11' class='hi'/><circle cx='230' cy='110' r='11' class='hi'/><circle cx='70' cy='220' r='11' class='hi'/><circle cx='230' cy='220' r='11' class='hi'/><text class='lbl ink' x='146' y='45'>2</text><text class='lbl ink' x='66' y='115'>1</text><text class='lbl ink' x='226' y='115'>3</text><text class='lbl ink' x='66' y='225'>5</text><text class='lbl ink' x='226' y='225'>4</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Five vertices 1-5 with edges 1-2, 2-3, 1-3, 1-5, 3-4, 4-5 and the diagonal 3-5.

given.aim: Write the neighbour set of every vertex from the drawn edges.

terms.example: 2 at top, 1 left, 3 right, 5 bottom-left, 4 bottom-right.
terms.connects: Each gets one row in the adjacency-list table.

terms.example: {3,5} is the diagonal from the right-middle vertex to the bottom-left vertex.
terms.connects: Each edge contributes one entry to each of its two endpoints' lists.

terms.meaning: The list lengths: deg(1)=3, deg(2)=2, deg(3)=4, deg(4)=2, deg(5)=3.
terms.connects: The handshaking check validates we captured all edges.

given.plan: Read each of the seven edges off the figure, place each endpoint in the other's neighbour list, then sanity-check with the handshaking lemma.

steps.title: List the edges
steps.apply: From the figure: 1-2 and 2-3 (the top triangle's slanted sides), 1-3 (the horizontal middle), 1-5 (left vertical), 3-4 (right vertical), 5-4 (bottom), and 3-5 (the diagonal). That is 7 edges.
steps.note: List every edge once as an unordered pair before building the lists.

steps.title: Build each vertex's neighbour set
steps.formula_raw: for each {u,v}: add v to adj[u] and u to adj[v]
steps.note: Each undirected edge appears in exactly two lists.

steps.title: Verify with the handshaking lemma
steps.apply: Degrees are 3,2,4,2,3 summing to 14 = 2 x 7, matching the 7 edges. The representation is complete and consistent.
steps.note: A quick correctness check that no edge was missed or double-counted.

questions.formula_note: Building adjacency lists is direct edge bookkeeping; the handshaking lemma (sum of degrees = 14 = 2 x 7) confirms all seven undirected edges were captured.

formulas.name: Chromatic Number of a Bipartite Graph
formulas.plain: chi(G) = 2 for a bipartite graph that has at least one edge; chi = 1 if it has no edges.
formulas.whenToUse: When a graph has no odd cycle (is bipartite / two-colourable), e.g. grids, trees, hypercubes, even cycles.

terms.means: chromatic number, the minimum number of colours in a proper colouring

formulas.trap: An edgeless graph is technically bipartite but needs only 1 colour; bipartite implies chi <= 2, not exactly 2, unless an edge exists.
formulas.reference: Bipartite graph theorem (a graph is 2-colourable iff it has no odd cycle)

formulas.name: Hypercube Q_n: Chromatic Number and Diameter
formulas.plain: The n-dimensional hypercube Q_n is bipartite so chi(Q_n) = 2, and its diameter equals n (the maximum Hamming distance).
formulas.whenToUse: When a graph's vertices are length-n bit strings adjacent iff they differ in one bit (the hypercube / n-cube).

terms.means: chromatic number, equal to 2 (parity of bit-sum gives the 2-colouring)
diameter, equal to n (distance = Hamming distance, max between complementary strings)

formulas.trap: Do not confuse the diameter n with the degree (also n) or with the number of vertices 2^n; the diameter is the longest shortest path.
formulas.reference: Properties of the n-cube graph Q_n

formulas.name: Odd Cycle Forces At Least Three Colours
formulas.plain: If a graph contains a cycle of odd length it is not bipartite, so it needs at least 3 colours.
formulas.whenToUse: When an otherwise bipartite figure (grid, even cycle) gains an extra edge that creates an odd cycle.

terms.means: a closed walk with an odd number of distinct edges

formulas.trap: The converse direction: chi >= 3 holds with any odd cycle, but reaching exactly 3 still requires exhibiting a valid 3-colouring.
formulas.reference: Konig's theorem / bipartite characterisation (no odd cycle iff 2-colourable)
